title: Translation, Rotation & Scaling
concept: Translation, rotation, and scaling are the three basic geometric moves you can apply to an image — shifting, turning, and resizing its pixels without changing what they depict.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What do classical filters like blur, sharpen, Sobel, and Canny all have in common?

options.text: They all slide a small, fixed kernel across the image and do the same repeatable local math at every position
They all require a trained neural network to run
They only work on grayscale images

exercises.explain: Right — every one of those filters uses a fixed, hand-designed kernel. Now let's look at moves that reposition a whole image instead of reading its local neighborhoods.

cards.title: Three ways to move a picture
cards.body: Grab a photo on your phone and you can slide it sideways, spin it, or pinch to zoom. None of those actions change what's in the photo — they just change where and how it sits. Those three moves are exactly where geometric image transforms start.

cards.title: Translation, rotation, and scaling
cards.body: Translation shifts every pixel by the same amount left/right and up/down. Rotation turns the whole image around a fixed point by some angle. Scaling grows or shrinks the image by a factor, moving pixels closer to or farther from a center point. Each one repositions pixels — none of them invents new image content.

cards.title: Rearranging furniture, not remodeling
cards.body: Think of a rug on a living room floor. Sliding it to a new spot is translation. Spinning it 30° is rotation. Buying a bigger rug in the same shape is scaling. The rug's pattern never changes — only its position, angle, or size in the room.

cards.title: Steadying shaky camera footage
cards.body: Video stabilization software watches a shaky clip and, frame by frame, translates (and sometimes slightly rotates) each frame to cancel out the camera's jitter — nudging every frame back toward a common, steady position.

cards.title: These don't change image quality
cards.body: It's tempting to think rotating or moving an image somehow degrades it like a filter would. In their pure form, translation and rotation just relocate pixels — the main practical costs are that some areas may end up empty (revealing blank space) or content can shift outside the frame and get cut off.

cards.title: Same content, different placement
cards.body: Three moves, one shared trait: nothing about the picture's content changes.

exercises.prompt: You spin an image 45° around its center. Which transform is this?
exercises.explain: Correct — turning an image around a fixed point by an angle is rotation.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Translating an image changes the size of the objects inside it.
exercises.explain: False. Translation only shifts pixel positions left/right/up/down — object size stays exactly the same.

exercises.sentence: Scaling moves every pixel closer to or farther from a center point, making the whole image ___ or shrink.
exercises.explain: Grow is correct — scaling up moves pixels outward from the center, enlarging the image; scaling down does the reverse.

exercises.prompt: Which of these describes a pure translation?

options.text: Every pixel in the photo shifts 20 pixels to the right and 10 pixels down, nothing else changes
The photo is stretched to twice its original width

exercises.explain: A uniform shift with no turning or resizing is exactly what translation means.

exercises.prompt: A photo-editing app lets you drag an image to reposition it, use a wheel to spin it, and pinch to make it bigger or smaller. What do all three controls have in common at the pixel level?

options.text: Each one repositions existing pixels according to a rule (shift, angle, or distance from center) without inventing new image content
Each one requires the app to guess new colors that were never in the original photo
Each one only works after the image has been converted to grayscale
Each one deletes part of the image permanently, the same way cropping does

exercises.explain: Exactly — translation, rotation, and scaling all just move pixels around according to a geometric rule. Next: what happens when you deliberately keep only part of an image, or change its overall dimensions — cropping and resizing.
